speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. My name is Barbara Hollander. I'm interviewing Abraham Getman, July 10th, 1996. Mr. Getman, I was asking you before about your-- you've told us a very, very remarkable story filled with courage-- your own personal courage. When you look back on your experiences, what is a lesson that you would want future generations watching this video to come away from, to learn from your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, was taken away, a big part of my life. I lost my teenagers. I'm living with my nightmares. It's not time [INAUDIBLE] even now. People still, to this day, didn't learn the lesson. You see what's going on even in Bosnia, where people killing each other for just name from a religion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Ireland-- Irish killing Irish.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all the same people. We all the same blood. It's 90%, I think is blood, and 10% is water. If you only have different-- you only have different customs that's the only reason between all humanity, why should it happen? I couldn't tell the story until-- I couldn't talk to my children. They never ask me even, 'What happened to my grandmother, grandfather?' My brother got killed in an accident, so his daughter wrote me once, called me up, and asked me, 'What happened to the family? Where is my grandfather?' [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was alive, they didn't ask him. My brother's children didn't ask him. No one is there to come and asking me. The same thing-- my children, maybe they will start to ask me after I'm gone. I decide to come out now while I'm alive. I'm like, the Spielberg story, I'm the last witnesses. Younger than me, don't remember anything. Older than me, they're already dead. And I'm one of the last witnesses. And I hope that people should lesson the world is made for everybody and start [INAUDIBLE] each other and live in peace and harmony. I hope they'll get the message that I myself don't hate people after all what happened to me. I'm not looking for revenge. I'm just looking people should understand and make a better living. And there's-- there's enough room for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much for sharing this extremely moving story with us. I would very much like to see pictures that you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will. It's a pleasure. And thank you for having me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us about this picture, Mr. Getman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Abraham Hetman-- Getman, and, 1939. I was 13 years old. Bar mitzvah in the back of my house after the ceremony in the temple. In the big synagogue. With a prayer book.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who do we see in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right is my father, Shmuel Tzvi, and my mother, Miriam. This is probably early stages, I don't know how old, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early stages of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Their marriage, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you please tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in 1936 before my brother left for Palestine, in the backyard from my grandfather's house. To the right is my sister Faigie. Standing, my brother, Moshe, my father, Shmuel Tzvi. Next to the left is the grandmother Sorah. Above the grandmother Sorah is Chaim and my little brother Leibel. Then my mother and between, this is me, Abraham Getman. And left, Yaakov Hetman. This picture also taken before my brother leaving for Palestine, but is combined with-- in the middle is my grandfather and my grandmother. And to the left is my mother, Miriam. To the right is of my mother's sister Faigy with her six children. And my mother with the other side of the family with six children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my family. And only above-- my grandfather, the tall man in the middle, is my mother's brother Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and your sister's family's last-- your mother's sister's family's last name? What was their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gornik.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Israel Gornik, Faigy Gornik, and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is, my home, where I was born. We had a shoe store on the front with the two big chimneys, I mean, was heating up the big house. And in the center is, the synagogue, what's about 600 years old. And the middle is our neighbor, [? Stern. ?] And in the front, supposed to be the market, what's nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the name of this synagogue was?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't ever name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, the town synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: The schul-- the Gross' schul.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Gross' schul. OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and can you tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is south side from the schul. And a-- a house on the south side. And you see there this, and-- there's a-- a box with the sanitation garbage we used to put in the left hand corner-- corner there. Underneath was stables and warehouses. And in the middle, the roof, we used to play. We used to sleep when it was hot in the summer. Go out, settle in this roof and, sleep in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this picture from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this from-- a-- the foundation-- the Luboml Foundation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is this brave-looking soldier we're looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me, Abraham Getman, there in the Israeli IDF. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1948, '49, '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you were a soldier then in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, and what is this we're looking at with Hebrew words?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the town in-- Jerusalem, I think, near King David Mountain. And a stone in memory of our town. And-- and now from the Luboml and the neighborhood. And [INAUDIBLE]. And brought us, what they got killed, murdered, and buried alive through the, murders, Germans, Ukraine, in 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Their memory from the ghetto Luboml in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took a photograph of this stone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I was in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. In what year did you take this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, probably in the 50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this beautiful family picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the wedding for my daughter Francine to Adam Berman. And to the left is my son Steven. This is me, Abraham, next one. And my wife, Muriel. My daughter, the bride, Francine. My son-in-law, Adam. My younger daughter, Arlene. And Mark to the right near the canopy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding-- a couple years ago, I think. '52, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: '92, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Beautiful picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Getman, can you introduce us to your beautiful family that's here now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To my right is my wife, Muriel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right and back of me is, my daughter Francine, and my daughter Arlene. And-- [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: Steven.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Steven. They're all Getmans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. I guess I'd like to address this to your family. Your husband and your father has told us a remarkable story filled with a lot of courage and inner strength. I'd like to give each of you an opportunity to say something on the tape about your father's experiences and what it's meant to you. Mrs. Getman, do you want to begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his experiences have taught me a lot what happened in a different country. Because I was born in the United States, and the time that he was reliving all these experiences, I was just having a grand time as a child. And he wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's sort of like a comparison, speaking. He lived one type of a life, which was very sad and tragic, and I was having fun at the same time in the United States. Also, it gives us an opportunity to realize that hopefully, this will never, never happen again. And perhaps it'll go down in the record that it shouldn't happen again. OK, thank you. Who would like to go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Francine?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's the oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'm not going next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arlene?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not ready yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Having assisted my father in editing the book, and listening to his words, and having to put some emotion of my own into it, I've learned a lot about the value of family and how important it is. My father being the sole survivor of a very large family, that my own siblings and this family is very special. And I respect them all and, you know, appreciate every minute I have to spend with them. And also, a lot of things that I've learned through the story, too, is the story about tolerance. And how important it is to tolerate people of different backgrounds and races, and to accept people for their differences, because we're all equal in God's eyes. And I think lastly, from my father and just from his struggles, he's a strong person. And it's always-- he always had hope. And we can-- we should always as a people, and as individuals, have hope for the future. And hope that people in the future will always respect everybody, and let people live their lives, and practice their religions, and always, just respect just-- just life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. I just wanted to ask you something. You mentioned the book that you helped your father with. Could you just very briefly tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been a long time that my father took to write and to edit it. He has a very unique story. I've, you know, read a lot and heard a lot about the Holocaust just being Jewish myself. But from his perspective, to see the things that a young boy goes through that are almost inhumane and just incredulous. It's hard to even for me, to believe that my father, who I know now, as, you know, somebody here in this country, went through these experiences there that are so just-- just abominable and just shocking that I just-- I've had to-- It's been hard to even edit it and touch this material, because it just-- it-- it brings a lot of emotion to me. And I-- I now understand better what happened there. And I just-- I don't know if I could have survived what he did, because it actually-- it's just-- it's just-- it should not happen to anybody. And I just can't believe people could do that to anybody else. And I think if you were to pick up the book, that you would understand, how just special he is as a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who'd like to go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Francine?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I had gone to Europe, well, when I graduated college. And on our trip, we did go to Germany, and we did see the Dachau camp. And when we went, there weren't many of us that were Jewish on the trip, and we begged the people to let us stay there. And going right in front of the camp and seeing the tracks, and knowing what my father went through, I was, like, actually a witness and standing right there where all these people died right in front of Jewish people, gypsies. And when you stood there and watched the films, but seeing it in person is different than hearing it even from the person that you know the most. And you're hearing it from strangers and reading it, but being right there and touching the tracks and going on the railroad car-- it's a completely different experience than anything I've ever, you know, imagined. Also, I helped-- before my sister even started with the book, I had helped started typing it. And since my father's not too clear in his English, we had, to, like, literally translate out proper English for him. Because he was saying it more in his words, and we would try to put it together piece by piece, and putting it all together. And then he's-- a few years, he's been honored by the [INAUDIBLE] Society and all the people that have been in the villages. And you see all the older men and their wives, and everybody who's gone through it with him. And he has a good, dear friend who lives in the next town who has a number on his arm. And we've even-- you know, seeing that, you read about people having numbers on their arms. You don't actually know many people around today who still have a number on their arm. That's like, a true eyewitness of something that happened to somebody. And they go on in spirit, but in actuality, it's still deep down in their heart that they, you know, still have feelings for what went on. And when they all get together, they-- you know, they get very sad and emotional. But we try to bring them out of it. He's very-- my father's very strong, very willed. And all of a sudden within the past 10 years, he started speaking to colleges and local high schools, and different organizations to tell his story. And even the kids nowadays are more in tune to what's going on, and they ask him questions. And he's willing to answer them honestly. When I was in high school, he wouldn't do this. He didn't bring out his story. When my brothers went, I think they-- their class--

speaker: interviewee
text: Junior high.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- junior high, he volunteered it. So it shows, within a 10-year difference, how he was willing-- it took me longer to get his thoughts out in the open, instead of keeping them, deep, hidden, roots hidden. And it's better, because too many people have hidden what went on, and, you don't hear--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who died.

speaker: interviewee
text: --And died. So now, even with everybody that's come out with the movies and everything, it's the best thing that's ever happened. And the memory has to live on for all of us, our generation, the generation after us, and after us. Otherwise, it's a lost chapter in history.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Steven. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I just want to say something about the concentration camps. A lot of people, even Jewish people-- they assume that somebody went through the Holocaust, that they had to have been in a concentration camp. [INTERPOSING VOICES] And when they hear that they're not in a camp, all of a sudden, it's like, oh, it's not a story. But they don't understand that these ghettos and these other places--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you're last. I'm last.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, we-- also, like my two sisters, I've also helped with the book. I remember when I--

speaker: interviewee
text: We both helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: We both helped with the book. When-- when we were younger, we tried typing as best as we could. And we were younger, and we didn't know, you know, typing that well. And we were trying just to understand it and read it. And over the years now with the computer and getting more education, and knowing the computer, and typing better, we could help him decipher it and put it in a better track and then give it to my sister, who finalizes it. And we still like to help do it--

speaker: interviewee
text: A team.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could-- I could go back. I found the tapes where he actually spoke into the tapes, and you could hear it. And it's very hard to listen to the tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The emotion.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the emotion, and then to put it on words. Because my father, some of his words do not come out-- [LAUGHING] they come out, you know, hard to understand. But we finally understand what he wants to say, and we put it in a-- in a better context. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But from working-- and working-- I've worked with my father for the past five years. You get to know, hear more when-- when his friends come over and talk about the Holocaust and everything, and he talks about more. And he-- he's telling everybody that comes in and he's friends with, 'Wait, my book's coming out. I'm going to finish it, it's going to be done. That's-- I'm devoting all my time.' And he doesn't listen to me. I said, 'Take all the time you want. Don't-- you don't have to come into work.' And I want him to finish the book, but my father taught us a lot of strength and a lot of unity with the family. And family comes first. We're a small family, but to each other, we all mean strength--

speaker: interviewee
text: In numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strength in numbers. We all mean a lot. Like, even though we're six, we have the strength of 60. [LAUGHTER] And we want to keep the-- the legacy and-- and the-- the strength of the Getman family on forever. And I went to Israel, and I met, um, my relatives. But I didn't get to meet his brother, which I know I heard a lot of. And I heard a lot about my father from-- from my aunt. And I, you know, that's one thing that-- that I wanted to do, was to meet my grandparents. And, you know, if I could have, I would have liked to meet them, because, you know, with the time that they had, they raised a great, great son.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll take the blame, as long as-- [LAUGHING] [INAUDIBLE] you kids, I'll take all the blame.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no blame.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess you saved the best for last. [LAUGHING] First of all, I'm looking forward-- I'm going with my father next month to the Ukraine, which was originally Poland when-- where he was born. And I'm looking forward to this very much, because I'm going to take some video and take some pictures, and try to visualize it, just when I take the pictures, of how it was then when he grew up, and-- and trying to see how it changed. And from what I've heard, it hasn't changed much. But also see how he grew up, the towns where he survived, the towns where he fled to, and get a stronger feeling of the conditions when he was our age, 13 or younger. And, I'm looking forward to that. And I was in Israel in Yad Vashem. And before that, he would tell us the story, I would read the story, type it, write it out. It's not the same when you go to Yad Vashem, and you see all the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to the Holocaust Museum in-- in Washington, DC. And it hit me a lot more, because with his story, and the stories there, and the pictures there, it-- it just all-- it comes together. And it makes a whole picture of, how did he survive from all these atrocities and everything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I'm very proud of my father, and he has a lot of strength. And, I'm glad you survived, because, [LAUGHING] if you, you know-- it-- it-- it's a miracle. And like he says, it's a miracle. And it really is. And he has a lot of strength and courage. And he-- from what he learned throughout that time, he passed onto us to be strong and not to have any differences against anyone. And for that, I'm really proud of and glad to-- I'm proud to-- my father was in the Israeli army, and I'm proud people tell me. And I'm proud to have a father [LAUGHING] that I have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I just say something regarding the book? I took his writings, and I deciphered it before anyone else put it into print. And I deciphered it, sort of compromised it, and put a 35-page mini-book, into effect. And I had to do it for a project, and I got an A-plus. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we just want to wish him a-- happy birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: We wish him happy birthday to you!

speaker: interviewee
text: Number 70!

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING] Happy birthday to you!

speaker: interviewee
text: We all love you!

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING] Happy birthday, dear Abraham. Happy birthday to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to kiss the full head.

speaker: interviewee
text: One more.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. That was, very moving and very beautiful. Thank you.
